coredata.dc:title: Electrochemically deposited Ag structure-based ECL sensing platform for KRAS gene detection in the tumor tissues
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Peilin
Wang, Zizhun
Li, Zhenrun
Wang, Yuan
Ma, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor was fabricated to detect the mutant KRAS gene in the tumor tissues. Firstly, a new kind of chitosan-based dots was prepared and regulated by phenylboronic acid (PBA) as ECL nanoprobe. Furthermore, the electrochemically deposited Ag structures with controllable morphology have been developed on the electrode. The detailed electrochemical deposition condition of Ag structure has been investigated deeply. Due to the synergistic effect of the large active surface area and the high conductivity, the layered Ag flower-like structure with branches can significantly enhance the ECL intensity of CS-PBA dots by 12.1 times. Finally, the toehold mediated strand displacement strategy was employed in the ECL biosensor to quantitatively detect mutant KRAS gene with the range of 0.01 pM to 10 nM. The limit of detection was 3.3 fM. This biosensor has been used successfully to analyze the target DNA in the tumor tissues of actual cancer patients. The results showed the novel sensing platform possessed great potential for clinical analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Ag flower-like structure
Controllable electrochemical deposition
KRAS detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CS dots, CS-PBA dots and CS-PBA dots/probe DNA
Controllable electrochemical deposition of Ag structures
Analytical performance of the ECL biosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Graduate Innovation Fund of Jilin University
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Program for Jilin University Science and Technology Innovative Research Team

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22174051 ), the science research project (No. JJKH20211050KJ ) in the Education Department of Jilin Province , China and the Graduate Innovation Fund of Jilin University .

The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22174051), the science research project (No. JJKH20211050KJ) in the Education Department of Jilin Province, China and the Graduate Innovation Fund of Jilin University. The use of human tumor tissue samples has been approved by the ethics committee of the First Hospital of Jilin University (No. 2021-162).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CS-PBA dots, (B) FTIR spectra of CS dots and CS-PBA dots, (C) FL spectra of CS dots and CS-PBA dots, (D) ECL intensities of CS dots and CS-PBA dots.
SEM images of Ag structures under different electrochemical deposition voltage: (A) − 0.1 V; (B) − 0.2 V; (C) − 0.3 V; (D) − 0.4 V; (E) − 0.5 V. The deposition time was 400 s.
SEM images of Ag structures under different electrochemical deposition time: (A) 200 s; (B) 300 s; (C) 400 s; (D) 500 s; (E) 600 s. The deposition voltage was − 0.3 V.

The ECL intensities of CS-PBA dots with different Ag structures under different electrochemical deposition voltages (A) and times (B). The ECL enhancement of CS-PBA dots with different Ag structures electrochemically deposited under different voltages (C) and times (D).

(A) EIS of (a) GCE, (b) Ag FS/GCE, (c) DNA duplex structure/Ag FS/GCE, and (d) CS-PBA dots/probe DNA/DNA duplex structure/Ag FS/GCE. (B) The relationship between the ECL intensity and different concentration of target DNA. (C) The corresponding linear relationship for target DNA detection. (D) ECL intensities of target DNA, wild type KRAS gene, 138 A alle mutant KRAS gene, 134 A allele mutant KRAS gene and blank assay. Error bars represent the standard deviations of three independent measurements.
Illustration of the Ag FS-based ECL platform with TMSD strategy.
Determination of target DNA in human colorectal cancer tumor sample and paracancer sample lysate.

head.ce:title: Electrochemically deposited Ag structure-based ECL sensing platform for KRAS gene detection in the tumor tissues

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electron Microscopy Center, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electron Microscopy Center, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor was fabricated to detect the mutant KRAS gene in the tumor tissues. Firstly, a new kind of chitosan-based dots was prepared and regulated by phenylboronic acid (PBA) as ECL nanoprobe. Furthermore, the electrochemically deposited Ag structures with controllable morphology have been developed on the electrode. The detailed electrochemical deposition condition of Ag structure has been investigated deeply. Due to the synergistic effect of the large active surface area and the high conductivity, the layered Ag flower-like structure with branches can significantly enhance the ECL intensity of CS-PBA dots by 12.1 times. Finally, the toehold mediated strand displacement strategy was employed in the ECL biosensor to quantitatively detect mutant KRAS gene with the range of 0.01 pM to 10 nM. The limit of detection was 3.3 fM. This biosensor has been used successfully to analyze the target DNA in the tumor tissues of actual cancer patients. The results showed the novel sensing platform possessed great potential for clinical analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is one of the most common malignant tumors of the digestive system. Colorectal cancer has a high fatality rate and causes more than 880,000 deaths each year [1,2]. As a proto-oncogene, the mutation and activation of the KRAS gene are closely related to the occurrence and development of colorectal cancer [3]. KRAS gene mutation is an unfavorable factor in the occurrence, development and prognosis of colorectal cancer, which is closely related to the efficacy of targeted therapy. The most common type of KRAS gene mutation occurs in codons 12 and 13 [4]. So, the evaluation of mutant KRAS is critical to the diagnosis and prognosis of patients with colorectal cancer. The expression level of related biomarkers and genes in tumor tissues is closely related to the development, metastasis, and other malignant biological behaviors of cancer [5]. The analytical methods were currently applied in clinical research, including quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR), mutant-enriched PCR and next-generation sequencing (NGS). Despite the advantages of these methods, these methods were constrained by high cost, long turnaround time and complicated steps [6,7]. Therefore, new sensing methods for the quantification of the KRAS gene in tumor tissue samples are urgently needed.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a light emission process involving the substance undergoing high energy electron transfer reaction and forming excited state on the surface of electrode, which possesses the advantages of both spectroscopy and electrochemistry [8,9]. ECL sensing systems have demonstrated excellent analytical performance in biomarker detection and cancer diagnosis due to the low background noise, high sensitivity, and precise process control [10]. Currently, many nanomaterials with excellent optical and electrochemical properties have been employed as ECL emitters, such as semiconductor nanocrystals [11], quantum dots (QDs) [12], gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) [13] and so on. Among these ECL luminophores, carbon-based nanomaterials including carbon dots [14], graphene QDs [15] and polymer dots [16] have received widespread attention. The carbon-based nanomaterials have unique chemical characteristics, including low toxicity and good biocompatibility. The abundant functional groups make the functionalization and modification of the carbon-based nanomaterials more flexible [17]. Meanwhile, the surface states of the carbon-based luminophores have a decisive effect on ECL performance [14]. However, the low ECL efficiency of carbon-based nanomaterials still limited the ECL sensing application. Until now, many studies with nanotechnology and sensing strategies have been explored to improve the ECL intensity of luminophores. On the one hand, new kinds of carbon-based nanomaterials were prepared as ECL luminophores. For example, element doping has been widely used to improve the optical properties of carbon-based nanomaterials. Doping with heteroatoms (such as N, S and P) can effectively increase the electrochemical activity and adjust the band gap of carbon dots [18]. Organic carbon-based nanoparticles with AIE-ECL characteristics were also synthesized with high ECL efficiency [19,20]. On the other hand, ECL sensing strategies have been reported in the sensing application, such as coreaction accelerator, ECL-RET and surface enhanced ECL [21]. The ECL intensity of luminophores can be significantly enhanced on the modified sensing interface. The sensing interface integrates with signal transduction and specific biorecognition, which provides superior electrochemical features owing to rapid mass transport and excellent conductivity. Recently, gold nanostars, nanocubes and nanodendrites have been reported to be used as electrochemical and ECL enhanced materials [22–25]. Compared with spherical nanoparticles, these metal nanomaterials with anisotropic structures have strong enhancement ability. The tips and branches of the anisotropic structures have a large active surface area for analyte immobilization, which can increase electron transfer rates and the luminescence signal to improve detection sensitivity [25,26]. Especially, the noble metal materials grown in situ on the electrode surface were expected to improve the sensing interface performance. However, the precise regulation of the morphology and anisotropic structure of nanomaterials is still an important challenge with significant research value.

In this work, a novel electrochemically deposited Ag structure-based ECL sensing platform was developed for mutant KRAS gene detection in tumor tissues. A novel chitosan-based dots were synthesized as ECL probes via microwave assisted hydrothermal synthesis method. With the regulation of phenylboronic acid (PBA), the luminescence efficiency of chitosan-based dots (CS-PBA dots) was significantly improved. Furthermore, as shown in Scheme 1, Ag structures with different morphologies were prepared and investigated by the electrochemical deposition process on the electrode surface. Due to the synergistic effect of the large active surface area for the analyte immobilization and the high conductivity, the layered Ag flower-like structures (Ag FS) enhanced the ECL intensity of CS-PBA dots by 12.1-fold. Based on the electrochemically deposited Ag FS on the electrode, the ECL biosensor was constructed to detect the mutant KRAS gene. In the sensing system, toehold-mediated strand displacement (TMSD) was employed as the fast enzyme-free nucleic acid amplification strategy to reduce background noise and improve detection sensitivity [27,28]. The formed duplex DNA structures based on the template DNA, assisted DNA and protected DNA were immobilized on the Ag FS with Ag-S bond. The first TMSD was initiated when target DNA was added to this sensing system. Then, assisted DNA was displaced because of the hybridization of target DNA in the toehold region. Another toehold region in the middle of the template DNA was exposed and hybridized with CS-PBA dots/probe DNA to complete the second TMSD. The released target DNA can be hybridized with the first toehold region again and achieve the recycling process. The feasibility of the ECL biosensor was further verified by detecting target DNA in the patients' actual tumor tissue lysate with satisfactory results.

Carboxymethyl chitosan, PBA, N-ethyl-N′-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Silver nitrate (AgNO3), citric acid, sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), hydrogen diphosphate sodium (Na2HPO4), sodium phosphate (Na3PO4) and sodium thiosulfate (K2S2O8) were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dithiothreitol (DTT) was obtained from BBI Life Sciences (Shanghai, China). The oligonucleotides were synthesized and purified by Qingke Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). The human colorectal cancer tumor tissue samples were received from the First Hospital of Jilin University.

TEM images were obtained with JEM-2100 F field emission electron microscope operating at 200 kV acceleration voltages. The SEM images were obtained on Hitachi SU8020 field emission scanning electron microscope. Fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption were respectively acquired by F-2700 Fluorescence spectrophotometer and Hitachi UH5300 UV–visible spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was measured on a Thermo Nicolet 360 FTIR spectrometer using KBr pellets. The cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and electrochemical deposition were performed using CHI 660B electrochemical workstation. The ECL measurements were carried out with the ECL20 electroluminescent instrument from Guangzhou Ingsens Sensor Technology Co. Ltd. The electrochemical and ECL experiments were carried out based on three-electrode system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CS dots, CS-PBA dots and CS-PBA dots/probe DNA
ce:section.ce:para: 300.0 mg carboxymethyl chitosan was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water. Under continuous stirring, the turbid solution became clear. Then, 20.0 mg PBA was added into the above solution. In the microwave reactor (1000 W), the mixed solution was heated at 170 °C for 60 min. Then, the obtained dark brown product was dialyzed for 12 h with the dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000 Da). The final product (CS-PBA dots) was stored in 4 °C for further use. The synthesis process of CS dots was consistent with the above experimental steps without PBA. After being activated with EDC and NHS, the probe DNA was incubated with CS-PBA dots for 2 h to form CS-PBA dots/probe DNA. The above solution was dialyzed for 4 h to remove the unreacted probe DNA.

The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with aluminum oxide powder and cleaned by ultrasonication in ultrapure water and ethanol respectively. The GCE was immersed in the mixed solution containing 1 mM silver nitrate and 0.1 M citric acid. The electrochemical deposition process was carried out in three-electrode system via constant voltage at − 0.3 V. The deposition time was 400 s. The Ag structures with different morphologies were obtained by adjusting the deposition voltage and the deposition time.

10 μM template DNA, 10 μM assisted DNA and 10 μM protected DNA were incubated together at 95 °C for 10 min. The incubation solution was gradually cooled to room temperature to form DNA duplex structures. Then, 0.1 mM dithiothreitol (DTT) was added in the solution. The solution was incubated for 1 h to break the disulfide bond of SH-DNA. Then, 6 μL of the above duplex DNA structures were dropped on the surface of electrodeposited Ag FS-modified GCE. After being incubated for 6 h, the electrode was washed with buffer and then immersed in the mixed solution containing 50 μL of CS-PBA dots/probe DNA and 50 μL of different concentration of target DNA. The TMSD recycling process was completed within two hours at room temperature. The ECL signals were measured in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH=7.4) containing 50 mM potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) as coreactant. The scan potential was varied from − 1.5–0 V. The voltage of photomultiplier tube was set to 900 V.

The TEM image in Fig. 1A displayed that the monodisperse CS-PBA dots had spherical characteristics and a uniform diameter of ca. 20 nm. FT-IR spectra were used to characterize the functional groups of CS dots and CS-PBA dots. The results in Fig. 1B proved that PBA successfully participated in the structure of CS dots during the synthesis process. It can be clearly observed that CS-PBA dots and CS dots had similar functional groups. The characteristic peak in the range of 3200 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H. The sharp peak at 1619 cm−1 came from the bending vibration of N-H, which indicated that the amino group was on the surface of CS-PBA dots. The peaks at 1305 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1 respectively corresponded to the stretching vibration of C-N and C-O-C. More importantly, the partial cyclic structures derived from chitosan were retained in the structure of CS dots. Meanwhile, the peaks located at 1379 cm−1 were attributed to the B-O bond in the CS-PBA dots. It indicated that PBA molecules were indeed combined in the structure of CS dots.

The major optical properties and crystal information of CS-PBA dots have been studied in Fig. S1-S5, including the UV–vis spectrum, XRD patterns, fluorescence spectrum and ECL performance. CS-PBA dots presented better luminescence characteristics than CS dots. The UV–vis spectrum of CS dots and CS-PBA dots in Fig. S1 displayed the absorption peak at 265 nm caused by π-π electron transitions. XRD patterns in Fig. S2 had a relatively broad diffraction peak at 2θ= 21°, indicating that the CS dots had the amorphous carbon structure [29]. Compared with CS dots, the fluorescence intensity of CS-PBA dots significantly increased to 1.6 times (Fig. 1C). There was a red-shift of 10 nm in the emission spectrum of CS-PBA dots due to the presence of boron atoms generating the surface defect states [30,31]. In aqueous solution, PBA can easily undergo substitutional reaction with the hydroxyl group of polyol compound. The resulting B-O bond had the potential to form reversible cyclic boronic acid esters [32,33]. It was attributed to the presence of more surface defects and emission sites [34]. The boron atom in the PBA molecule was in the sp2 hybrid state with the electron deficient feature. It could be used as an electron acceptor. Therefore, the addition of PBA in the synthesis process introduced sufficient holes on the CS dots surface. The FL intensity was enhanced due to the increased non-radiative recombination efficiency and defect state emission [35,36]. In Fig. S3, CS-PBA dots exhibited the excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence characteristics. The fluorescence emission spectrum displayed gradual red-shift under different excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 380 nm. The ECL response of CS-PBA dots was investigated with 50 mM K2S2O8 in Fig. 1D. CS-PBA dots had a 1.4-fold higher ECL intensity than CS dots. Fig. S4 displayed the ECL emission spectra of CS dots and CS-PBA dots. The ECL emission peaks were at 570 nm and 590 nm, respectively. The red-shift of fluorescence and ECL spectra further proved the regulation of surface state for CS dots by the introduction of PBA. Since ECL was strongly dependent on the state of the luminophores surface, the addition of PBA made CS dots more sensitive to the electron injection process and the generation of active free radicals [14]. The surface capturing centers of excitons in the CS-PBA dots can capture more active free radicals. As a result, it exhibited significant ECL efficiency [12]. The ECL-potential curves in Fig. S5 A showed the excitation potential of CS-PBA dots shifted from − 1.39 V to − 1.33 V with the regulation of PBA. The possible ECL mechanism was discussed as below:
(1) CS-PBA dots + e- → CS-PBA dots•-
(2) S2O8 2- + e- → SO4 •- + SO4 2-
(3) SO4 •- + CS-PBA dots•-→ CS-PBA dots* + SO4 2-
(4) CS-PBA dots* → CS-PBA dots + hv

CS-PBA dots and the co-reactant S2O8 2- were reduced to CS-PBA dots•- and SO4 •-. Subsequently, CS-PBA dots•- reacted with SO4 •- to generate the excited state CS-PBA dots* . Finally, CS-PBA dots* relaxed to the ground state and generated intense ECL emission. The reduction peaks of CS-PBA dots and CS dots were located at − 1.20 V and − 1.35 V respectively in Fig. S5B. The low reduction peak potential indicated that CS-PBA dots were reduced more easily to generate the excited state CS-PBA dots* [37]. Due to the regulation of PBA molecules, the efficiency of charge injection and radiation recombination was improved. As a result, the ECL intensity of CS-PBA dots increased obviously. The CS-PBA dots with high ECL efficiency were used as excellent ECL probes for the following biological analysis research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Controllable electrochemical deposition of Ag structures
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of Ag structures were precisely controlled by tuning the applied electrochemical deposition conditions. Electrochemical deposition was a simple and useful route to nanomaterial formation which controlled the nucleation and growth process of Ag structures by tuning the electrochemical deposition conditions. The size, particle distribution and density of Ag structures can be regulated effectively [38,39]. A typical morphology evolution was investigated by the electrochemical deposition potential from − 0.1 V to − 0.5 V. As the applied voltage gradually decreased, it can be seen that Ag structures changed from small spherical particles to layered flower-like structures with branches, eventually forming the packed sphere-like aggregation structures ( Fig. 2). When the electrochemical deposition voltage was − 0.1 V, only a few spherical nanoparticles were obtained (Fig. 2A). When the electrochemical deposition voltage was − 0.2 V and − 0.3 V, Ag FS with three-dimensional multilayers and obvious branches were formed (Fig. 2B, C). With the voltage decreased to − 0.4 V and − 0.5 V, the tips of the flower-like layered structure nearly became rounded (Fig. 2D, E). The effect of more electrochemical deposition voltages (higher than −0.1 V or lower than −0.5 V) was also investigated. The SEM images in Fig. S7 showed only nanoparticles and cluster structures were formed when the voltage was higher than − 0.1 V. And there was no layered Ag FS on the electrode surface when the voltage was lower than − 0.5 V. It indicated that the electrochemical deposition voltage played a key role in the controllable electrochemical deposition of Ag structures. The formation of the layered noble metal nanostructures was usually influenced by the limited diffusion of metal ions near the electrode, the strength of the electric field and the growth of metal atoms on the corresponding crystal faces [40–42]. During the preparation of Ag structures, as the applied voltage changed, the intensity of the electric field affected the diffusion of silver ions and guided the growth of nanostructures along the direction of the electric field [38]. In addition, the high concentration of silver ions near the tips led to the vertical growth of the flower-like layer.

The effect of electrochemical deposition time on Ag structures was also investigated in the range from 60 s to 1200 s. The SEM images in Fig. 3 and Fig. S8 indicated that the electrochemical deposition time mainly affected the density and size of Ag structures. When the deposition time was 60–200 s (Fig. 3A, Fig. S8A, B), only spherical NPs were formed due to the limited growth time. As the deposition time increased, the average size of the nanoparticles gradually increased from 50 nm to 130 nm. With the time extension from 300 s to 600 s, nanosheets were prepared and formed the layered flower-like structures. When the electrochemical deposition time reached 600 s (Fig. 3E), the density of Ag FS increased significantly. A wide distribution of Ag FS was observed on the electrode surface. With the change of electrochemical deposition time from 300 s to 400 s, the diameter length of Ag FS increased. Then, the size of Ag FS decreased when the electrochemical deposition time exceeded 400 s. The curve of size change with electrochemical deposition time was shown in Fig. S10. When the electrochemical deposition time approached 600 s, the aggregate structures of Ag nanoparticles were obvious. It indicated the Ag FS were not generated under too long growth time.

Fig. 4 displayed the ECL responses of CS-PBA dots with different Ag structures. As the morphology of the Ag structures changed, there were different ECL enhancement effects of CS-PBA dots. The Ag FS had distinct tips and branches with the electrochemical deposition voltage at − 0.3 V in Fig. 2C. Therefore, the platform developed with Ag FS displayed excellent large active surface area for the analyte immobilization. Meanwhile, the UV–vis spectrum of Ag FS has been displayed in Fig. S11. Because of the synergistic effect of the large active surface area and the high conductivity [40], the ECL intensity of CS-PBA dots was increased by 12.1 times with Ag FS in Fig. 4A. Fig. 4B exhibited the enhanced ECL intensity of CS-PBA dots with Ag structures which were formed by controlling the electrodeposition time from 100 s to 600 s. It indicated the density and size of the Ag FS obtained under the electrochemical deposition time of 400 s, which made the maximum ECL enhancement of CS-PBA dots. As a result, the electrochemical deposition was performed under a constant potential at − 0.3 V for 400 s as the optimal conditions to fabricate Ag FS. It was employed for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: As the highest mutation rate in accordance with the previous reports [4], the G12D type mutation of KRAS was associated with the poor prognosis of colorectal cancer patients. Tumor cells with this mutation type were more capable of transformation. The patients had shorter recurrence time and poorer survival [43,44]. So, this type of KRAS mutant gene was detected in the sensing system as an effective index to evaluate the early diagnosis and efficacy evaluation of cancer.

Fig. 5A displayed the interfacial properties of the electrode during the sensing process. After Ag FS were electrochemically deposited on the electrode, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) was 563.4 Ω (curve b). Due to the non-conductivity of DNA molecules, when the DNA duplex structure was connected with Ag FS on the electrode, the value of Rct increased to 1173.4 Ω (curve c). Then the Rct significantly increased to 2079.1 Ω after the hybridization and cycling processes were completed. The gradual increase in the value of Rct indicated the immobilization of target DNA and probe DNA on the electrode, which proved the successful establishment of the sensing system. Meanwhile, the polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) analysis shown in Fig. S12 also verified the feasibility of the sensing strategy. In order to get stable ECL signals and improve the sensitivity of the biosensor, the incubation time during the TMSD displacement process and the coreactant concentration have been optimized in Fig. S13. As shown in Fig. 5B, the ECL intensity increased accordingly with the addition of target DNA. In Fig. 5C, the logarithm of the concentration of KRAS gene (from 0.01 pM to 10 nM) displayed a linear relationship with the ECL intensity of CS-PBA dots, which fitted as I= 5.36 × 103 Log c + 4.26 × 104 (R2 =0.986). The detection limit was 3.3 fM. Moreover, the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the ECL biosensor were tested in Fig. 5D. The results showed the biosensor was able to distinguish the target DNA successfully. Compared with other methods for detecting KRAS gene previously, this work had satisfactory analytical performance (as shown in Table S2). Furthermore, the colorectal cancer tumor tissues and paracancerous tissues were detected as actual samples. The detection results in Table 1 demonstrated that this ECL biosensor had the ability to detect the target DNA in the actual samples of tissue lysate. The recoveries in the tissue lysate were in the range of 93–103%. It indicated the target KRAS mutant gene was highly expressed in colorectal cancer tumor tissue.

In this work, the ECL biosensor has been constructed with CS-PBA dots and Ag FS. CS-PBA dots were synthesized with microwave assisted hydrothermal method as ideal ECL emitters. Ag FS were electrochemically deposited on the electrode surface. The Ag FS with layered structure and branches provided the synergistic effect of the large active surface area and the high conductivity. It can amplify the ECL intensity of CS-PBA dots by 12.1 times. The sensing system has been employed in the detection of mutant KRAS gene in the colorectal tumor tissue samples successfully. In order to construct ideal ECL biosensors, the high degree of controllability of the morphology of gold and silver materials needs to be further explored.

Peilin Wang: Investigation, Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Zizhun Wang: Investigation, Supervision, Resources. Zhenrun Li: Visualization, Investigation. Yuan Wang: Methodology, Visualization, Formal analysis. Qiang Ma: Supervision, Project administration, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22174051), the science research project (No. JJKH20211050KJ) in the Education Department of Jilin Province, China and the Graduate Innovation Fund of Jilin University.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of human tumor tissue samples has been approved by the ethics committee of the First Hospital of Jilin University (No. 2021-162).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132212.

.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Global cancer statistics 2018: GLOBOCAN estimates of incidence and mortality worldwide for 36 cancers in 185 countries

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Is KRAS mutation associated with interval colorectal cancers?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Shaukat, M. Arain, R. Anway, S. Manaktala, L. Pohlman, B. Thyagarajan, Is KRAS Mutation Associated with Interval Colorectal Cancers?, Digestive Diseases and Sciences, 57(2012) 913–917.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Frequency and type of KRAS mutations in routine diagnostic analysis of metastatic colorectal cancer

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Expression of human DNA methyltransferase 1 in colorectal cancer tissues and their corresponding distant normal tissues

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of KRAS G12D point mutation level by anchor-like DNA electrochemical biosensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Zeng, J. Xiang, Detection of KRAS G12D point mutation level by anchor-like DNA electrochemical biosensor, Talanta, 198(2019) 111–117.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Increased detection sensitivity for KRAS mutations enhances the prediction of Anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody resistance in metastatic colorectal cancer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Molinari, L. Felicioni, M. Buscarino, S. De Dosso, F. Buttitta, S. Malatesta, et al., Increased Detection Sensitivity for KRAS Mutations Enhances the Prediction of Anti-EGFR Monoclonal Antibody Resistance in Metastatic Colorectal Cancer, Clinical Cancer Research, 17(2011) 4901–4914.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemically generated versus photoexcited luminescence from semiconductor nanomaterials: bridging the valley between two worlds

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Bai, L. Shan, B. Xie, X. Huang, W. Mao, X. Wang, et al., Mutations in KRAS codon 12 predict poor survival in Chinese patients with metastatic colorectal cancer, Oncol Lett, 15(2018) 3161–3166.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Peilin Wang is a postgraduate student in Jilin University. Her research interests are largely focused on electrochemiluminescence and surface plasmon coupling electrochemiluminescence.
Zizhun Wang works in Electron Microscopy Center, Jilin University. His main research direction is the transmission electron microscopy characterization method of nanomaterials.
Zhenrun Li is an undergraduate student in Jilin University. Her current scientific interests are electrochemiluminescence and novel electrochemiluminescence nanomaterials.
Yuan Wang is an undergraduate student in Jilin University. His research interest is on the preparation of novel nanomaterials.

Qiang Ma is an Associate Professor of Jilin University. His research interests cover the novel electrochemiluminescence nanomaterials, electrochemiluminescence biosensors, surface plasmon coupling electrochemiluminescence and their applications in cancer diagnosis.
